mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 130i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 130i (N52B30) engine typically costs £1,500–£3,500 supply only. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on location and workshop rates. Used engines start from £800–£1,800 supply only. Main dealer new engines start from £6,000–£10,000+ supply only.

Yes — the N52 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable and characterful petrol engines. With proper maintenance, these engines regularly exceed 150,000–200,000 miles. However, common issues include electric water pump failure, oil leaks from gaskets, and Valvetronic system problems.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 130i?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Electric water pump failure — can fail without warning, (2) Oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket, and (3) Valvetronic system issues — causing rough idle, reduced power, or stalling.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 130i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £4,000–£8,000 130i, a £2,500–£3,500 recon is 30–85% of the car's value. For a clean example with good body and no rust, replacement may be worthwhile given the 130i's strong following as a future classic.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 130i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 130i uses the N52B30 engine family. Engine codes include N52B30A (early 265 PS), N52B30AF (early 258 PS), N52B30B (LCI), and N52B30BF (LCI). The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Does the N52 have a cambelt or timing chain?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 has a timing chain, not a cambelt. The chain is designed to last the life of the engine and does not require routine replacement. Unlike the N47 diesel, the N52's timing chain is not a common failure point. However, the VANOS system can suffer from cam ledge wear, which is an expensive repair.

problems.title: Electric Water Pump Failure
problems.symptoms: Engine overheating, coolant warning light, heater blowing cold air, electric fan running constantly at high speed

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: overheating can cause catastrophic engine damage

problems.recommendation: The N52 uses an electric water pump that is a known failure point. It can fail without warning, leading to rapid overheating. One owner reported: "Water Pump. Failure anywhere from 50,000 onwards, no warning". Preventative replacement is strongly recommended when the car reaches 60,000–80,000 miles, especially if the coolant system shows any signs of weakness. Replace the thermostat at the same time. If the temperature gauge rises or the electric fan runs constantly, stop driving immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full water pump failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing Gaskets)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, oil spots under the car, visible oil residue on the engine block, low oil level warning
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (gasket replacement — valve cover or oil filter housing)
problems.recommendation: The N52 engine is prone to oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket. These leaks can lead to oil loss, contamination of engine components, and if left unaddressed, can cause engine damage. A valve cover gasket replacement is a straightforward but labour-intensive job. The oil filter housing gasket is a known weak point — the gasket degrades and leaks oil down the front of the engine. If you notice a burning oil smell or oil spots beneath the car, have the gaskets inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide → /failures/oil-leaks-n52/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Valvetronic System Issues
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, reduced power, hesitation on acceleration, stalling, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (Valvetronic motor, eccentric shaft sensor, or wiring repair)
problems.recommendation: The Valvetronic system — BMW's variable valve lift technology — can develop problems over time. The eccentric shaft sensor, Valvetronic motor, or wiring can fail, leading to rough idling, reduced power, or stalling. Diagnosing Valvetronic problems requires specialist knowledge and diagnostic equipment. The VANOS system can also suffer from cam ledge wear, which is an expensive repair. If you experience rough running, have the system diagnosed by a specialist before the problem worsens.

failureLink.label: Read the full Valvetronic failure guide → /failures/valvetronic-system-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 130i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using BMW-spec oil
* Evidence of recent water pump and thermostat replacement
* No oil leaks — or documented recent gasket replacement
* Good body and chassis condition — these are ageing performance cars
avoidIf: * Overheating history or cooling system issues — the N52 is unforgiving of overheating
* Visible oil leaks from the front of the engine (oil filter housing gasket)
* Rough idle or hesitation that hasn't been diagnosed — could be Valvetronic
* Poor service history — the N52 is reliable but needs proper maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 130i's N52 is one of BMW's finest naturally aspirated engines — a 3.0-litre inline-six with a characterful soundtrack, linear power delivery, and genuine longevity. The 130i is the most powerful 1 Series of its generation, offering 265 PS and a 0-60 time of 6.0 seconds. The engine is generally robust, but the electric water pump, oil leaks, and Valvetronic system are well-documented weak points. Buy with full service history and evidence of preventative maintenance, and you'll have a future classic that rewards every drive.

cta.label: → Compare 130i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 130i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks; ask about water pump replacement history
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the valve cover and oil filter housing
* Cold Start — Listen for any rattling, ticking, or rough running on start-up
* Service History — Including the N52 engine and confirmed oil change intervals
* Valvetronic Health — Any rough idle, hesitation, or stalling?
* VANOS — Any loss of low-end power or flat spots? Could indicate cam ledge wear
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for Valvetronic or VANOS codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
